title: Render-Blocking Resources
intro: A page shows a blank white screen for 3 seconds before anything appears. The HTML is fine and the server responds quickly -- the problem is in the <head> tag. Render-blocking resources are preventing the browser from showing anything. Find the line causing the worst delay.

teach.title: Render-Blocking = Traffic Jam at the Gate
teach.body: Imagine a concert venue where everyone has to go through a single gate. Even though thousands of people are ready, only one line moves at a time. **Render-blocking resources** work the same way -- the browser finds a CSS file or JavaScript file in the <head> and stops everything to download and process it before showing any content to the user.

teach.title: Render-Blocking = Reading the Entire Manual Before Cooking
teach.body: Imagine you want to make pasta. Instead of just starting to boil water, you insist on reading the entire 200-page cookbook cover to cover first. Nothing happens in the kitchen until you're done reading. That's what **render-blocking resources** do to your page -- the browser insists on fully loading certain files before it shows the user anything, even though most of that content isn't needed right away.

teach.title: What Blocks Rendering?
teach.body:
Two types of resources block rendering by default:

1. **CSS files** -- The browser won't show anything until all CSS in the <head> is loaded, because it doesn't want content to flash unstyled
2. **JavaScript files** -- Scripts in the <head> without 'async' or 'defer' block HTML parsing completely

The more blocking resources you have, the longer users stare at a blank screen.

teach.title: Blocking vs Non-Blocking
teach.code:
<!-- BLOCKING: Browser stops everything to load this -->
<script src="/heavy-script.js"></script>

<!-- NON-BLOCKING: Browser continues while loading -->
<script src="/heavy-script.js" defer></script>
<script src="/analytics.js" async></script>
teach.annotation: The 'defer' attribute downloads the script in parallel and runs it after HTML parsing. The 'async' attribute downloads in parallel and runs as soon as it's ready.

teach.body: A blank white screen for 3 seconds is all it takes for a user to hit the back button and find someone else's app. Eliminating render-blocking resources is one of the fastest ways to keep users -- and their wallets -- on your page.

Render-blocking resources (CSS and JS in the <head> without async/defer) force the browser to stop and wait before showing anything. Add 'defer' or 'async' to scripts, and only put critical CSS in the <head>.

exercise.prompt: Four JavaScript files load in the <head> without async or defer, blocking all rendering. The biggest offender is a massive utility library loaded synchronously. Which line causes the worst delay?
exercise.explanation: Loading the full unminified Lodash library synchronously in the <head> is the worst offender. It's a large file that blocks everything. Adding 'defer' lets the browser download it in parallel while continuing to parse the HTML and show content. Option B (minified) is smaller but still blocking. Option C removes functionality you might need. Option D changes it to a module, which defers by default but may break non-module code that depends on it. The cleanest fix is 'defer', which downloads in parallel and runs after HTML parsing.
